Were You Asked This?
Design a URL Shortener

Design a URL shortening service similar to TinyURL that allows users to convert long URLs into short, shareable links and manage their shortened URLs.

Asked at:

PayPal

Microsoft

JPMorgan Chase

L

lloyds

+15 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

A URL shortener is a web service that converts long, unwieldy URLs into short, shareable links and tracks basic engagement. Think of TinyURL or Bitly: you paste a long URL, get a compact code like https://sho.rt/Ab3Cd, and anyone hitting that short link is redirected to the original address.

Interviewers ask this because it looks simple but exercises core distributed systems skills: globally unique ID generation without collisions, extreme read scaling (redirects), low latency at the edge, write-heavy analytics capture, abuse prevention, and solid data modeling. It’s a perfect canvas to see if you can define crisp requirements, estimate scale, choose the right storage and caching strategy, and make pragmatic trade-offs around availability, consistency, and cost.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
Bitly

System design answer key for designing a URL shortener like Bitly, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

View Answer Key

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to create a short link from a long URL.

Users should be able to use a short link to be redirected to the original URL with low latency.

Users should be able to manage their links (view, disable/delete, optionally update destination) via an authenticated dashboard.

Users should be able to see basic analytics for each link (total clicks, time series, referrers, country/region).

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you generate globally unique short codes at high write rates without collisions or hotspots?

How will you achieve low-latency redirects worldwide (p95 < 50–100 ms)?

How will you capture click analytics without affecting redirect latency?

How will you prevent abuse (spam, phishing, brute force) and keep the service reliable?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Reads

Redirect traffic is overwhelmingly read-heavy, often orders of magnitude higher than writes. Meeting global low-latency SLAs requires aggressive caching (Redis/edge), geo-distribution, and cache-invalidation strategies.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Short code creation and analytics event ingestion are write paths that must scale horizontally. You need write-friendly data models, append-only event streams, and techniques like sharded ID generation to avoid bottlenecks.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Naive global counters or uniqueness checks can create hotspots. Handling contention via sharding, pre-allocation, conditional writes, and region-scoped namespaces is essential to keep code generation fast and reliable.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis is ideal for ultra-fast short-code→URL lookups, rate limiting, and approximate real-time counters. It provides sub-millisecond access, simple data structures, and can be deployed per region to minimize latency.

Read More
DynamoDB

A managed, highly available key-value store fits the core mapping (code→URL) and link metadata. DynamoDB offers predictable low-latency reads/writes, auto-scaling, conditional writes for uniqueness, and global tables for multi-region.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka decouples the redirect path from analytics by providing durable, scalable ingestion of click events. It supports at-least-once delivery, back-pressure handling, and downstream stream processing for aggregations and dashboards.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Bitly

It is the same class of system: short-code generation, low-latency redirects, heavy caching, and an analytics pipeline for clicks and referrers.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

Both ingest high-volume click events, require durable event pipelines, and compute time-windowed aggregates without blocking user-facing requests.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Abuse prevention in a public URL shortener needs per-tenant and per-IP throttling at scale, mirroring the design of distributed rate limiters.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Using a single auto-increment database counter for code generation

This creates a hot primary key, contention, and a single point of failure. Under load or across regions it will throttle writes and hurt availability.

Serving redirects directly from the primary datastore without caching

Ignoring edge/Redis caching will miss latency targets and explode costs at scale. It also increases blast radius during incidents and makes hotspots more likely.

Performing synchronous analytics writes on the redirect path

Coupling I/O heavy logging to the user request raises tail latency and failure correlation. A backlog or partial outage in analytics would break redirects.
